'''Clifford Eccles''' (born 4 September 1967 in [[Warrington]]) is a professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 1980s, 1990s and 2000s. He played at representative level for [[Ireland national rugby league team|Ireland]], and at club level for [[Blackpool Borough|Springfield Borough/Chorley Borough]], [[Rochdale Hornets]], [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]], [[Swinton Lions]] and [[Widnes Vikings]], as a {{rlp|pr}}, or {{rlp|sr}}.<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/>

==Playing career==
Eccles started his professional [[rugby league]] career with [[Blackpool Borough|Springfield Borough]], signing from amateur club [[Leigh Miners Rangers|Leigh Miners]] in 1987.<ref>{{cite news |title=Salford sign Aussie ace |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0004985/19870829/035/0035 |work=Manchester Evening News |date=29 August 1987 |page=35 |via=[[British Newspaper Archive]] |url-access=subscription}}</ref> He usually played as a {{rlp|sr}} forward during the early years of his career, but made a successful switch to {{rlp|pr}} forward during the [[1989–90 Rugby Football League season|1989–90 season]].<ref>{{cite news |title=Eccles puts on the style |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0004985/19900113/067/0067 |work=Manchester Evening News |department=Pink Final |date=13 January 1990 |page=11 |via=[[British Newspaper Archive]] |url-access=subscription}}</ref>

Eccles spent the [[1991–92 Rugby Football League season|1991–92 season]] on loan at [[Rochdale Hornets]], playing for the club in the [[1991–92 Lancashire Cup]] final defeat against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]]. He joined the club on a permanent basis for the following season.<ref>{{cite news |title=Eccles signing boosts Hornets |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0004985/19920810/034/0034 |work=Manchester Evening News |date=10 August 1992 |page=34 |via=[[British Newspaper Archive]] |url-access=subscription}}</ref>

Eccles was signed to Salford at the start of the 1994 season from Rochdale Hornets by the then head coach [[Garry Jack]]. He went on to play a key role in the side that achieved Grand Final Success at [[Old Trafford]] in 1996, and was awarded the [[Tom Bergin Trophy]] for his man of the match performance.<ref>{{cite book |editor-last1=Fletcher |editor-first1=Raymond |editor-last2=Howes |editor-first2=David |title=Rothmans Rugby League Yearbook 1996 |date=1996 |publisher=Headline Publishing Group |location=London |isbn=978-0-7472-7767-5 | page=209}}</ref> He went on to play in the [[Super League]] under the guidance of former [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] player [[Andy Gregory]].

Gregory's team made three Challenge Cup semi-final appearances but never quite stepped up to reach the Wembley Final, the most memorable being at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley Stadium]] against the [[Sheffield Eagles]], with minutes to go Salford were in charge of the game, Gregory decided to replace Cliff Eccles and [[Andy Platt]] in the front row, this proved to be his tactical downfall as the Sheffield Eagles went on to score tries in the very gaps that the two strong front rowers had left in the defensive line.

Eccles held the record at the Salford Reds for most consecutive appearances by a forward at 103 without missing a game over four seasons including Super League and Challenge Cup Games.

Cliff left the Salford Reds before the start of the 1999 season to join former Salford player turned coach [[Mike Gregory]] in his first [[head coach]]ing role at [[Swinton Lions]].

Eccles stayed with Gregory at Swinton for one season and then joined the [[Widnes Vikings]] with [[Colin Whitfield]] as head coach; after one season Eccles retired from the game with damaged knee ligaments.

==International honours==
Cliff Eccles won [[Cap (sport)|cap]]s for [[Ireland national rugby league team|Ireland]] while at the Salford City Reds 1997–1998 1-cap + 2-caps (sub).<ref>{{cite book|first1= Graham|last1= Williams|first2= Peter|last2= Lush|first3= David|last3= Farrar|title= The British Rugby League Records Book|publisher= London League|isbn= 978-1-903659-49-6|year= 2009|pages= 104–105}}</ref>

==Personal life==
Cliff Eccles is the younger brother of the former [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] rugby league international, [[Bob Eccles]].<ref>{{cite web |title=First final at Wilderspool since Bishop's red card shocker |url=https://www.warringtonguardian.co.uk/news/5233442.first-final-at-wilderspool-since-bishops-red-card-shocker/ |website=Warrington Guardian |access-date=2 January 2024 |date=14 November 2003}}</ref>
